title: SpaceX Orbital AI Data Centers, China's Rare Earth Squeeze, and Turkey's BYD Pullback
subhead: SpaceX unveiled massive AI satellites designed for orbital computing, China's rare earth export controls sent mineral prices soaring, and Turkey suspended tax breaks for BYD's stalled $1 billion factory plan — marking a day of significant shifts in AI infrastructure, critical mineral dependencies, and EV investment politics.

sections.heading: SpaceX Reveals AI Satellite for Orbital Data Centers
sections.body:
SpaceX publicly unveiled its first-generation AI satellite, dubbed AI1, designed to operate as an orbital data center with computing capacity far beyond traditional communications satellites. According to details released by the company and reported by 24/7 Wall St., the spacecraft features a 70-meter wingspan — nearly three-quarters of a football field — and up to 150 kilowatts of peak computing payload, with a massive 110-square-meter thermal radiator system for heat dissipation.

The timing coincides with SpaceX's preparation for one of the most anticipated IPOs in recent memory. The company has filed plans with the FCC for a constellation that could eventually include up to one million orbital data center satellites, according to Data Center Dynamics. Unlike Starlink satellites that prioritize communications, AI1 is designed for computing first — with solar arrays and cooling systems occupying most of the structure.

The engineering challenge is significant: space offers no air conditioning or liquid cooling, meaning heat must be radiated away. SpaceX's design suggests the company understands this constraint, but the economics of orbital computing versus terrestrial data centers remain unproven.
sections.whyItMatters: If successful, SpaceX could capture a slice of the trillion-dollar AI infrastructure market by moving compute off Earth — eliminating land constraints, grid bottlenecks, and permitting delays. The IPO adds urgency to demonstrating viable orbital economics.

sections.heading: China's Rare Earth Export Controls Cripple Western Supply Chains
sections.body:
China's April 2025 export controls on seven critical rare earth elements — yttrium, samarium, gadolinium, terbium, dysprosium, lutetium, and scandium — have devastated Western supply chains. According to Crypto Briefing analysis, yttrium oxide prices in European markets climbed to approximately $270/kg by late 2025, representing a 4,400% increase from 2024 levels when yttrium traded below $8/kg.

US imports of yttrium from China collapsed by roughly 95% — from 333 tons in the eight months before controls to just 17 tons in the corresponding period afterward. The controls don't technically ban exports but require special licenses that are nearly impossible to obtain.

In response to Chinese dominance, Japan announced on June 11 that Shin-Etsu Chemical will build a new rare earth refining facility in Fukui prefecture to strengthen supply chain resilience, Reuters reported. India and the US have also signed a framework agreement to secure critical mineral supplies and bypass Chinese dependence.
sections.whyItMatters: China controls roughly 90% of global rare earth processing capacity. The price shock and import collapse reveal how quickly Beijing can weaponize mineral dependence — and how long it takes democratic nations to build alternative processing infrastructure.

sections.heading: Turkey Suspends BYD Tax Breaks Over Stalled Factory
sections.body:
The Turkish government has suspended import tax exemptions for BYD and warned the Chinese electric vehicle maker that it faces repayments if it fails to fulfill a $1 billion commitment to build operations in the country, according to Nikkei Asia. Construction has not started on a plant that was meant to be operational this year.

Turkey's Minister of Industry and Technology Mehmet Fatih Kacir stated the government is closely monitoring BYD's progress on the planned factory in the western province of Manisa. Questions were raised in Turkish parliament over the lack of progress, reflecting growing political pressure on Chinese EV investments.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's harder line reflects growing scrutiny of Chinese EV investment promises across Europe. BYD's factory was meant to anchor Turkey's EV manufacturing base — the suspension signals Ankara won't wait indefinitely for results.

sections.heading: China Unveils AI Police Tech at Beijing Expo
sections.body:
An international police equipment exhibition in Beijing showcased Chinese AI surveillance technology capable of measuring physical health, mental state, and emotional conditions of suspects, according to the South China Morning Post. Tiandy, a video surveillance supplier based in Tianjin, demonstrated cameras that can assess vital signs including heart rate, blood pressure, blood oxygen level, and blood flow rate of up to six people simultaneously with over 90% claimed accuracy.

The three-day expo, which ended June 10, targeted both domestic and international police forces. Indonesia expressed interest in Chinese equipment, particularly counterterrorism gear, according to SCMP's reporting. The technology is designed for interrogation waiting areas to track detainees' live location and warn of medical crises.
sections.whyItMatters: Chinese surveillance tech exports continue to expand beyond facial recognition into emotional and psychological state detection — raising human rights concerns and offering authoritarian states new monitoring capabilities.

sections.heading: Ukraine-US Drone Defense Deal Takes Shape
sections.body:
The US and Ukraine have drafted a memorandum outlining terms for a defense agreement that would allow Ukraine to export military technology to the US and manufacture drones in joint ventures with American companies, according to CBS News. Ukrainian Ambassador to the US Olha Stefanishyna confirmed the draft framework is under review by both sides.

Ukraine has already signed defense agreements with Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the UAE over the past two months, with President Zelenskyy saying nearly 20 countries are involved at various stages. Ukraine's National Security Council projects a defense production capacity of $55 billion in 2026 — though Kyiv currently has funds for only about $15 billion in weapons purchases.

Ukrainian companies excel at manufacturing weapons systems the US hasn't prioritized. One Ukrainian manufacturer plans to produce over 3 million low-cost first-person-view military drones in 2026, compared to only 300,000 built by the US in 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone expertise — forged through years of war with Russia — is becoming an exportable strategic asset. The US-Ukraine deal could reshape defense industrial capacity for both nations.

sections.heading: Undersea Cable Infrastructure Faces Growing Threats
sections.body:
Iran has threatened to charge Big Tech companies transit fees for undersea internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz — a digital chokepoint carrying critical connectivity between Europe and Asia, the Straits Times reported. Iranian officials and state-linked media floated plans to demand billions in annual fees from Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon, with IRGC-linked media issuing veiled threats about potential cable damage.

The threat highlights a broader vulnerability: undersea cables handle 99% of international internet traffic yet receive minimal protection. The Red Sea saw a major disruption in February 2024 when a British vessel struck by a Houthi missile drifted and damaged three cables, disrupting a quarter of traffic between Asia, Europe, and the Middle East.

The Baltic Sea has experienced at least 13 cable damage incidents since October 2023, frequently involving aging vessels with opaque ownership — often shadow-fleet tankers or China-flagged bulk carriers with Russian links. In November 2024, the Yi Peng 3, a Chinese bulk carrier, dragged its anchor across roughly 300 kilometers of seabed, cutting two cable links.
sections.whyItMatters: Undersea cables are indispensable yet almost absurdly exposed. As geopolitical tensions rise, these digital arteries face increasing threats from state actors and plausible deniability operations.

bottomLine: June 11, 2026 marked significant moves in tech geopolitics: SpaceX's orbital AI data center ambitions signal a potential new frontier for compute infrastructure, China's rare earth weaponization demonstrates how quickly mineral dependence can become leverage, Turkey's BYD pullback reflects growing scrutiny of Chinese EV investment promises, and undersea cable vulnerabilities drew fresh attention as Iran threatened digital chokepoints. The connecting thread is infrastructure — who controls it, who depends on it, and how quickly it can be weaponized.
sources: 24/7 Wall St., Data Center Dynamics, Crypto Briefing, Reuters, Nikkei Asia, South China Morning Post, CBS News, Straits Times, Business Insider, Forbes, Yahoo News, DroneLife, Associated Press
